one_line_takeaway: Injecting a single crafted image-text pair into a multimodal RAG knowledge base can either force an attacker-chosen answer (up to ~57% success) or collapse all answers to zero accuracy, and standard paraphrasing/consistency defenses catch essentially none of it.
key_finding: Both attacks succeed severely and evade defenses. LPA reaches up to 46.4% attacker-answer accuracy (ACC_Pois) even in full black-box (LPA-BB) and up to 56.8% ACC_Pois with 88.8% poisoned-retrieval recall under retriever-only access (LPA-Rt) on MMQA. GPA is devastating with a single (or five) injected entries: original retrieval recall collapses to 1.6% on MMQA and 0.0% on WebQA, and original-answer accuracy drops toward 0. Under GPA-RtRrGen the poisoned entry achieves >90% top-1 retrieval recall vs 0.4% for the true context and the generator returns the attacker's answer with ~100% accuracy. LPA-Rt transfers strongly across retrievers (CLIP->OpenCLIP/SigLIP) without re-optimization, while GPA transfers more weakly. Poisoned images show 31.2% (MMQA) and 40.7% (WebQA) higher query-image similarity than true contexts. All tested defenses fail: paraphrasing leaves attack effectiveness roughly unchanged, and CLIP-based auditing detects 0% of adversarial entries while the multimodal abnormality detector reaches only ~10% for LPA-BB and <4% for GPA.
core_contribution: MM-PoisonRAG is presented by the authors as the first systematic framework for knowledge-poisoning attacks against end-to-end multimodal RAG (a pipeline of multimodal KB + CLIP-based retriever + MLLM reranker + MLLM generator). Its own novel contribution is two attack strategies: (1) Localized Poisoning Attack (LPA), a targeted attack that injects one query-specific but factually-wrong image-text pair to steer the generator to an attacker-defined answer; and (2) Globalized Poisoning Attack (GPA), an untargeted attack that injects a single query-irrelevant image-text entry engineered to look 'relevant' to every query, collapsing generation across the whole task. GPA is motivated by the authors' own geometric observation that image and query embeddings form separate clusters in CLIP's cross-modal space, so an adversarial image can be optimized toward the query-embedding centroid to dominate retrieval for all queries. This is distinct from the cited prior work (text-only RAG poisoning such as Zou et al. 2024, and image-to-image multimodal poisoning), which the paper explicitly argues cannot handle the text-query-to-image cross-modal retrieval setting it targets.
threat_model: Adversary has knowledge of the target QA task and can INJECT a limited number of adversarial image-text pairs into an open external knowledge base, but CANNOT modify or delete existing entries (emulating misinformation seeding in public sources). Four capability settings are studied (Table 1): LPA-BB (full black-box, no model access, 1 injected pair per query), LPA-Rt (white-box retriever access with gradients, 1 pair per query), GPA-Rt (retriever-only access, 5 injected entries for all queries), and GPA-RtRrGen (full white-box on retriever+reranker+generator but limited to a single injected entry for all queries). Attacker goals are either targeted misinformation (LPA) or wholesale generation collapse to nonsensical output like 'Sorry' (GPA). Transferability is also studied: adversaries with no access to the deployed retriever craft attacks on a surrogate (CLIP) and transfer to OpenCLIP/SigLIP.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: scope is narrow to knowledge-seeking QA tasks (summarization/dialog RAG untested); the paper focuses on designing/evaluating attacks rather than developing robust defenses (defense analysis is diagnostic only). Reviewer-noted: the abstract's headline 'four retrievers' is only partly evidenced in the main tables, which report CLIP-ViT-L as the operative retriever with transfer shown to OpenCLIP and SigLIP; LPA requires one injection per query (per-query cost), and it is notably weaker on WebQA where the generator ingests two contexts (m=2), giving a real context an opening to recover. The white-box GPA-RtRrGen and LPA-Rt settings assume gradient access that may be unrealistic for closed retrievers, though transferability results partly address this.

datasets_used: MultimodalQA (MMQA), filtered to 125 of 229 QA pairs that both LLaVA and Qwen-VL-Chat answer incorrectly without retrieval; single image-text context per query (m=1)
WebQA, filtered to 1,261 of 2,511 QA pairs; typically two contexts per query (m=2)
quantitative_results: LPA-BB (black-box): up to 46.4% attacker-answer accuracy (ACC_Pois) on MMQA (Qwen-VL-Chat generator, image+caption reranking)
LPA-Rt (retriever access): up to 56.8% ACC_Pois and 88.8% poisoned-retrieval recall (R_Pois) on MMQA (N=m, no rerank)
LPA-Rt on MMQA drops true-context recall to 8.8% (from 83.2 baseline, a 74.4-point drop) while poisoned recall reaches 88.8%
GPA-Rt: original retrieval recall collapses to 1.6% on MMQA and 0.0% on WebQA; original-answer accuracy falls to ~8.8% (MMQA) and ~12-13% (WebQA), down 42-50 points
GPA-RtRrGen on MMQA: poisoned context >90% top-1 retrieval recall vs 0.4% for the original context; generator returns attacker answer ~100% of the time
Poisoned images show 31.2% higher query-image embedding similarity on MMQA and 40.7% higher on WebQA than ground-truth images

Transfer (LPA-Rt CLIP->OpenCLIP, MMQA image+caption): up to 56.0% recall reduction and doubled poisoned-answer accuracy vs original; LPA-BB transfer to OpenCLIP causes 45.6% recall and 22.4% accuracy drops

Defense detection rates (Table 4): CLIP-based auditing 0% across all four settings; abnormality detection 10.4% (LPA-BB), 7.2% (LPA-Rt), 3.2% (GPA-Rt), 4.0% (GPA-RtRrGen); perplexity and feature-squeezing detectors ~0% at calibrated thresholds; paraphrasing defense leaves attack effectiveness essentially unchanged
baselines_compared: Retrievers: CLIP-ViT-L (primary/target), OpenCLIP, SigLIP (transfer targets); paper claims four retrievers total
MLLMs as reranker and generator: LLaVA and Qwen-VL-Chat (both roles evaluated)
Attack-content generators: GPT-4 (captions, with Mistral-7B as weaker ablation), Stable Diffusion (images, with DALL-E 3 / fewer-step SD ablations)

Defenses evaluated: paraphrasing (Jain et al. 2023 / Zou et al. 2024), perplexity filtering, feature-squeezing image detection (Xu et al. 2017), CLIP image-caption consistency auditing, combined multimodal abnormality detector (all at 5% FPR)
Reference/no-attack condition: pre-attack retrieval recall and accuracy (R_Orig, ACC_Orig baselines shown as red drop values in Tables 2-3)
